responses.text: The target Healthline article defines the Mandela effect as a situation in which a large group of people remember an event differently from how it occurred and frames it as a false-memory phenomenon rather than evidence of an alternate universe.
The page is a consumer health explainer written by Rachel Nall and medically reviewed by Timothy J. Legg, so it is useful for a general overview but is not itself primary scientific evidence.
The article’s core Nelson Mandela example is supported by primary evidence because the Nelson Mandela Foundation’s family statement says Mandela died on December 5, 2013, not in prison in the 1980s.

Independent psychological evidence supports the article’s broader point that memory can be distorted, because the American Psychological Association summarizes research showing that lineup instructions, prior photos, and feedback can affect eyewitness memory and identification.

A 2022 Psychological Science study on the visual Mandela effect found that some popular icons elicit consistent, specific false memories across people, which supports the article’s claim that shared memory errors can occur.

The Berenstain and Jif examples in the article check out against official sources, because the Berenstain Bears site identifies the creators and franchise as Berenstain and Jif says it has always been called Jif since its founding in 1958.

The Looney Tunes and Star Wars examples also check out against official sources, because Warner Bros. identifies the franchise as Looney Tunes and Lucasfilm says Darth Vader’s line is often misquoted as “Luke, I am your father” but is actually “No, I am your father.”

The main limitation is that the Healthline article makes several explanatory claims about confabulation and false memories in a simplified way without presenting a detailed research review or naming the doctors and researchers behind every claim.

Overall, the article is broadly trustworthy as a light consumer explainer because its major examples and its false-memory framing are corroborated, but readers should treat its medical-science discussion as introductory rather than definitive.
